segments.title: The Leander class in a navy between cruiser types
segments.summary: The Leanders appear as partially protected second-class cruisers while the Royal Navy searches for a practical replacement for costly dispatch vessels.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke places the four ships in a transitional Royal Navy that had bought speed in Mercury and Iris through expensive machinery without gaining corresponding fighting power. The Leanders combine cruising service and combat value, even though their classification shows how unsettled cruiser terminology remained.

evidence.note: The transcript introduces the policy background, classification, four-ship class, and service span.

segments.title: Six-inch guns, close defence, and Whitehead torpedoes
segments.summary: Projecting turntables, broadside weapons, small-calibre defence, and torpedoes gave the class flexible firepower.
segments.body: Four six-inch breech-loaders on projecting turntables received wide forward or after arcs, while two more fired through conventional broadside ports. Nordenfelt guns could depress against boats alongside, and four Whitehead tubes plus two carried torpedo boats threatened close attackers.

evidence.note: The transcript describes the battery, firing arcs, close-defence guns, torpedo tubes, and boats.

segments.title: Commerce protection turns into a cruiser programme
segments.summary: Board politics and commercial anxiety shape a cruiser meant to defeat unprotected raiders and deter stronger armoured cruisers with torpedoes.
segments.body: The design dispute outlasted a change of government because both political boards faced pressure to protect British trade. A Leander could not be expected to defeat an armoured cruiser alone, but it could damage one and overpower an unprotected cruiser.

evidence.note: The transcript follows the Smith, Northbrook, and Cooper Key debate into the commerce-protection rationale.

segments.title: Range and machinery for foreign-station work
segments.summary: A refined hull, large coal capacity, and landing-party weapons equip the class for long deployments despite modest engine power.
segments.body: Although heavier than Iris and fitted with less indicated horsepower, the Leanders achieved useful speed through better hull form and later reached 17 to 18 knots after rig and funnel changes. Their quoted 11,000-nautical-mile endurance and field guns reflect the range and independent duties expected of cruisers.

evidence.note: The transcript gives machinery, speed, coal, range, complement, and landing-party armament.

segments.title: How a thin protected deck kept the vitals alive
segments.summary: A sloped steel deck, coal bunkers, and subdivision protect machinery and buoyancy without a conventional armoured belt.
segments.body: The class concentrated a one-and-a-half-inch deck over magazines and machinery, with coal around the vital space as supplementary protection. Leander's grounding on Hornet Rock demonstrated the subdivision when she remained workable despite numerous sheared rivets.

evidence.note: The transcript explains deck armour, coal protection, subdivision, and the grounding damage.

segments.title: Commissioning HMS Leander day by day
segments.summary: Leander's first weeks reveal the practical work of turning a new cruiser into an operational warship.
segments.body: Captain Martin Dunlop's record follows magazine-flooding tests, compass adjustment, gunnery breakages, an engine-room fan defect, coal expenditure, and Whitehead torpedo trials. Commissioning appears as a demanding sequence of repairs and tests rather than a ceremonial handover.

evidence.note: The transcript reconstructs Leander's June 1885 commissioning from daily records.

segments.title: The Evolutionary Squadron makes peacetime practice matter
segments.summary: Leander joins the 1885 squadron that establishes realistic manoeuvres despite concern that hard use will wear ships out.
segments.body: Sir Geoffrey Phipps Hornby's squadron treated exercises as preparation for actual war and helped establish the Royal Navy's later manoeuvre system. Parliamentary criticism exposed the readiness dilemma: preserving ships reduced wear but left crews and fleet practice untested.

evidence.note: The transcript connects Leander with the squadron and debates exercise wear against readiness.

segments.title: Leander shifts from China Station cruiser to destroyer depot ship
segments.summary: Two China Station commissions lead into reserve, refit, and a new support role after Leander becomes obsolete as a cruiser.
segments.body: Leander served two commissions in Hong Kong and on the China Station before entering reserve and refit. In 1902 the Royal Navy converted the obsolete cruiser into a depot ship for torpedo-boat destroyers, extending the hull's value through a support role suited to a changing fleet.

evidence.note: The transcript traces Leander from two China Station commissions through reserve and conversion to a destroyer depot ship.

segments.title: Phaeton's trials expose the importance of boiler draught
segments.summary: Raised funnels and altered fire bars cure poor draught, allowing Phaeton to exceed contracted power and speed.
segments.body: Phaeton initially struggled to maintain steam, so her funnels were raised and fire-bar spacing changed. The revised arrangement delivered about 5,575 indicated horsepower and 18.64 knots at light draught, though a snapped component ended the intended six-hour run early.

evidence.note: The transcript gives Phaeton's draught changes, casualty, horsepower, speed, and official response.

segments.title: Phaeton's Nordenfelt accident demonstrates splinter danger
segments.summary: An accidental burst crosses Phaeton's deck and wounds four men through fragments thrown from internal structure.
segments.body: A charged four-barrel Nordenfelt was handed to Royal Marines and fired while being trained aft. Bullets penetrated iron beams and bulkheads, but the casualties came from splinters, showing how the ship's own structure could endanger its crew.

evidence.note: The transcript recounts the accidental firing and distinguishes bullet penetration from splinter wounds.

segments.title: Phaeton's Pacific service joins presence with navigational risk
segments.summary: Phaeton's overseas duty places a Leander-class cruiser in Britain's Pacific force and nearly ends in a fog collision near Victoria.
segments.body: Phaeton served as part of the Pacific Station's mixed cruiser, destroyer, sloop, and gunboat force. Her near collision with USS Iowa in dense fog illustrates that distant-station presence demanded routine navigation and seamanship as much as combat capability.

evidence.note: The transcript places Phaeton on the Pacific Station and recounts her near collision with USS Iowa in fog.

segments.title: Three Leanders provide a British Pacific presence
segments.summary: The 1897 Pacific force shows three ships of the class sustaining presence alongside an armoured cruiser, destroyers, sloops, and gunboats.
segments.body: Phaeton, Amphion, and Leander served together under Imperieuse in a mixed station force. Their concentration demonstrates the class's intended work: routine British presence, mobility, and support for smaller vessels far from home waters.

evidence.note: The transcript lists the Pacific Station force and three Leander-class cruisers.

segments.title: Amphion fights beyond the rules of the 1888 exercise
segments.summary: After escaping blockade with Rodney, Amphion attacks simulated shipping and coastal stations beyond exercise expectations.
segments.body: Amphion broke out from Lough Swilly and claimed shipping and coastguard targets along the British coast. Criticism that her actions did not follow the rules shows how controlled manoeuvre assumptions could diverge from wartime conduct.

evidence.note: The transcript describes Amphion's breakout, attacks, and disputed adherence to rules.

segments.title: George Warrender's path from Amphion to battle-squadron command
segments.summary: Amphion's 1888 officer list introduces George Warrender before his rise to senior squadron and shore commands.
segments.body: The ship's manoeuvre-era complement illustrates the mix of navigation, gunnery, engineering, medical, and warrant specialists needed to operate a cruiser. Dr. Clarke singles out gunnery officer George Warrender, who later commanded the East Indies Station and Second Battle Squadron and led a British visit to Kiel shortly before the First World War.

evidence.note: The transcript lists Amphion's officers and traces George Warrender's later commands and 1914 Kiel visit.

segments.title: Arethusa shows the economy and cost of ships in ordinary
segments.summary: A skeletal peacetime complement saves money, while repeated activations and China Station service reveal the mobilisation burden.
segments.body: Arethusa spent much of her early career with few officers before being commissioned for manoeuvres or station service. Her later place in the large 1901 China Station force shows both the economy of reserve practice and the layered overseas fleet for which such cruisers were mobilised.

evidence.note: The transcript contrasts reserve manning with manoeuvre, Mediterranean, Pacific, and China service.

segments.title: The 1888 blockade exercise rewards escape and raiding
segments.summary: Tryon's enemy fleet breaks Baird's blockade, while the known deadline distorts both sides' operational choices.
segments.body: The manoeuvre cast Ireland as hostile territory and asked Baird to contain Tryon's force while protecting British coasts and shipping. Tryon escaped after eleven days, but the predetermined end encouraged action a real commander might have delayed until the blockaders were exhausted.

evidence.note: The transcript explains the scenario, breakout, artificial deadline, and class members' results.

segments.title: Manoeuvres uncover stoker shortages and poor gun-platform behaviour
segments.summary: The inquiry links reserve practices to inexperienced engine-room staffing and identifies rolling, steering, and searchlight problems.
segments.body: Arethusa's captain wanted more artificers and trained stokers, revealing the personnel cost of leaving ships largely uncommissioned. Quick rolling made accurate shooting impossible in a beam sea, prompting proposals to reduce topweight, enlarge the rudder, improve bridge visibility, and cover the stern with a searchlight.

evidence.note: The transcript reports inquiry findings on staffing, rolling, rig, steering, visibility, and searchlights.

segments.title: Sail, coaling infrastructure, and the Leanders' design legacy
segments.summary: Incomplete coaling networks explain the retained rig, while the balanced protected-cruiser scheme becomes a model for later ships.
segments.body: Arthur Hood resisted removing sail because it conserved coal where foreign-station infrastructure was incomplete, making old technology useful insurance for a steam fleet. The class's protected vital box, shielded guns, endurance, and broad mission set then provided a coherent pattern scaled into later cruisers and smaller ships.

evidence.note: The transcript connects retained sail to coal access and assesses the class's influence on later designs.
